name: Almadenejos, Saceruela, and Abenójar Loop
name_original: Abenójar desde Almadenejos – circular por Saceruela y Fontanosas
description: This challenging 62.2-mile road cycling route from Almadenejos takes you through Saceruela and Fontanosas, with 3227 feet of elevation gain.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Almadenejos traverse a landscape shaped by the Valle de Alcudia and Sierra Madrona Natural Park. The region features varied terrain, from expansive plains with ancient oak trees to rugged mountains with significant elevation changes. Cyclists encounter well-paved surfaces suitable for road bikes, offering both flat stretches and challenging climbs through areas rich in historical mining heritage. This blend of natural beauty and industrial history provides a distinctive backdrop for road cycling. Best road cycling routes around Almadenejos The most popular road cycling route is Almadenejos, Saceruela, and Abenójar Loop, a 62.2 miles (100.1 km) trail that takes 4 hours 26 minutes to complete. This difficult route features substantial elevation gain through varied terrain. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Almadén from Veredas-Brazatortas – circular by Roman ruins of Sisapo, a difficult 79.8 miles (128.4 km) path. This route offers extensive riding through the natural park, passing historical sites and diverse scenery. Local road cyclists also love the Miner's Monument – Almadén Bullring loop from Almadén, a 51.9 miles (83.5 km) trail leading through areas with historical mining architecture, often completed in about 4 hours 9 minutes. Road cycling around Almadenejos is defined by expansive plains, rugged mountains, and historical mining landscapes. The network offers options for different ability levels, from challenging climbs to more rolling terrain. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 10 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Almadenejos's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes around Almadenejos?
A: The Almadenejos region, nestled within the Valle de Alcudia and Sierra Madrona Natural Park, offers a diverse road cycling experience. You'll find well-paved surfaces suitable for road bikes, ranging from expansive plains with ancient oak trees and pastures to rugged mountains with significant elevation changes. This blend provides both flat stretches and challenging climbs.

Q: Are there road cycling routes for all skill levels around Almadenejos?
A: While many routes in the Almadenejos area are quite challenging, there are options for varying abilities. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38158596" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almadén Bullring – Almadén Mining Park loop from Almadén&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate route. However, most routes, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1086174571/almadenejos-saceruela-and-abenojar-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almadenejos, Saceruela, and Abenójar Loop&lt;/a&gt;, are classified as difficult due to substantial elevation gains and longer distances.

Q: What historical sites or natural features can I see while road cycling near Almadenejos?
A: The region is rich in both natural beauty and historical mining heritage. You can cycle past areas with an 'impactful vision of the industrial history and the Manchegan mining landscape.' Notable attractions include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3901719" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almadén Mining Park&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5620080" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almadén Bullring&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24527466" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Miner's Monument – Almadén Bullring loop from Almadén&lt;/a&gt; specifically leads through areas with historical mining architecture.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes starting from Almadenejos?
A: Yes, several routes are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Examples include the challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1086174571/almadenejos-saceruela-and-abenojar-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almadenejos, Saceruela, and Abenójar Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the extensive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1085143036/almaden-from-veredas-brazatortas-circular-by-roman-ruins-of-sisapo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almadén from Veredas-Brazatortas – circular by Roman ruins of Sisapo&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there family-friendly road cycling options in the Almadenejos area?
A: The road cycling routes around Almadenejos are generally characterized by varied terrain, including significant elevation changes, making many of them more suitable for experienced cyclists. While there is a moderate route, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38158596" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almadén Bullring – Almadén Mining Park loop from Almadén&lt;/a&gt;, families with younger children or those seeking very easy, flat rides might find options limited. It's advisable to check the difficulty and elevation profiles of specific routes before planning a family outing.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Almadenejos?
A: The region's climate generally supports cycling for much of the year. Spring and autumn typically offer the most pleasant temperatures for road cycling, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the cooler, potentially wetter conditions of winter. These seasons provide ideal conditions to enjoy the expansive plains and challenging mountain climbs.

Q: Where can cyclists park their vehicles when starting a route in Almadenejos?
A: While specific parking areas for cyclists are not detailed, Almadenejos is a small town, and general parking can usually be found within or on the outskirts of the village. For routes starting from Almadén, parking facilities would be available in that town. It's always recommended to check local signage for any restrictions.

Q: Are there places to eat or stay along the road cycling routes near Almadenejos?
A: Almadenejos and other small towns within the Valle de Alcudia and Sierra Madrona Natural Park, such as Mestanza, offer local cafes, restaurants, and accommodation options. These can serve as convenient stops for refreshments or overnight stays, especially on longer routes. It's a good idea to plan your route to pass through these villages for breaks.

Q: Do any road cycling routes pass by rivers or reservoirs?
A: Yes, the natural park is crisscrossed by rivers like the Gargantiel and Valdeazogues, featuring clear waters and ash forests. The Montoro Reservoir is also a notable feature within the natural park and is often included in challenging cycling routes, offering picturesque scenery and water views.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy the most about road cycling in Almadenejos?
A: Road cyclists frequently praise the diverse and often quiet terrain, ranging from flat plains to significant mountain climbs. The unique blend of natural beauty and the historical mining architecture provides a constantly engaging and picturesque backdrop for cycling adventures. The minimal motor vehicle traffic on many roads, particularly mountain passes like Puerto Madrona, also contributes to an enjoyable and safe experience.

Q: Can I explore the historical mining heritage of Almadenejos on a road cycling route?
A: Absolutely. The region's rich mining legacy is a significant part of its character. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24527466" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Miner's Monument – Almadén Bullring loop from Almadén&lt;/a&gt; specifically lead through areas with historical mining architecture. You can see vestiges of the industry, including old foundries and the unique circular Baritel de San Carlos in Almadenejos itself.

Q: Are there options for longer, more challenging road cycling tours from Almadenejos?
A: Yes, the Almadenejos area is well-suited for longer and more challenging road cycling tours. Routes such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1085143036/almaden-from-veredas-brazatortas-circular-by-roman-ruins-of-sisapo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almadén from Veredas-Brazatortas – circular by Roman ruins of Sisapo&lt;/a&gt; cover nearly 80 miles with substantial elevation gain, providing extensive riding through the natural park and diverse scenery.
tips: An old mining town since Roman times, the town of Almadenejos is located on the border with the province of Córdoba, on the Almadén road in the Alcudia Valley.

Saceruela is a small rural town next to the CM-415 road. Rest in the Nuestra Señora de las Cruces Parish located in the Plaza de la Constitución. The town is one of the starting points of the historic route of the Camino de Guadalupe de Levante that connects the Iberian Peninsula with the town of Guadalupe in Cáceres.

The town of is one of the oldest towns in the area, at the service of pilgrims on the road to Andalusia. Today it sits on the C-424 highway that connects Puertollano with Almadén. You can stop to rest in the tree-lined Plaza de San Juan Bautista and admire the façade of the parish church, built with local stone and baked red brick.

faqs.question: What level of fitness is required for this racebike tour?
faqs.answer: This is classified as a "hard road ride" and requires very good fitness to complete. It covers approximately 100 kilometers with a significant elevation gain of around 980 meters.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this racebike tour?
faqs.answer: The tour is estimated to take around 4 hours and 27 minutes to finish, though this can vary based on individual fitness and pace.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this racebike tour?
faqs.answer: The route is designed for racebikes, meaning it primarily follows paved roads. However, as it traverses rural areas in Spain, the quality of the pavement can vary, so be prepared for potentially uneven surfaces in some sections.

faqs.question: Are there any notable towns or landmarks along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, the circular route connects several towns. You will pass through the Almadenejos Town Center, the town of Saceruela, and in Abenójar, you can see the Plaza de San Juan Bautista and Parish Church of San Juan Bautista.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this racebike tour?
faqs.answer: As this is a circular route starting and ending in Almadenejos, you can typically find parking within the Almadenejos Town Center or nearby areas. Specific parking facilities are not detailed, but street parking is often available in smaller Spanish towns.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to undertake this racebike tour?
faqs.answer: While specific recommendations for this route are not available, road cycling in the Ciudad Real province of Spain is generally best during spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October) to avoid the extreme heat of summer and the cooler, potentially wetter conditions of winter.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No, this racebike tour follows public roads and does not require any special permits or entrance fees to access.

faqs.question: Is this racebike tour dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: This is a racebike tour primarily on public roads. While dogs are generally allowed in public spaces, it is not suitable for cycling with dogs due to the distance, speed, and road conditions. It's best to leave your canine companions at home for this particular activity.

faqs.question: What should I bring for this racebike tour?
faqs.answer: Given the length and difficulty, you should bring appropriate racebike gear, plenty of water, nutrition, a repair kit, and sun protection. Consider carrying a phone for emergencies and a map or GPS device as rural areas may have limited signage.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes or places to refuel along the route?
faqs.answer: The route passes through several towns including Almadenejos, Saceruela, and Abenójar. These towns typically offer local cafes, bars, or small shops where you can stop for refreshments and to refill water bottles.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route briefly intersects with sections of the "Sendero Pantano-Charco Las Parras" (2% of the route) and the "Camino del Levante - Etapa 1" (1% of the route).

waypoints.name: Plaza de San Juan Bautista and Parish Church of San Juan Bautista, Abenójar
